card.title: Why We Laugh
card.teaser: Laughter isn't really about jokes. It's a 10-million-year-old social signal that predates language itself.
card.coverImagePrompt: A candid overhead shot of friends around a dinner table mid-laugh, golden warm light, genuine joy on every face, documentary photography style

lessons.title: What even is a laugh?
lessons.body: Try to describe a laugh without using the word 'laugh.' Involuntary rhythmic vocalization, uncontrollable breath spasms, a face that contorts on its own. It's weird when you think about it. And yet babies start doing it around four months old, deaf children laugh even without ever hearing one, and we're still not entirely sure why.

visual.imagePrompt: A baby mid-laugh with bright eyes and wide open mouth, soft natural light, tender and joyful

lessons.title: Laughter is older than jokes
lessons.body: Chimpanzees, gorillas, and even rats produce laughter-like vocalizations when tickled or play-fighting. Primate researcher Marina Davila-Ross traced the family tree of ape laughter and concluded it evolved from shared breathing patterns during rough play — at least 10 to 16 million years ago. Humans inherited the behavior; we just later wrapped it around language.

keyTerms.definition: Spontaneous, genuine laughter involving muscles around the eyes — hard to fake
Voluntary, social laughter used to signal friendliness or agreement, not genuine amusement

visual.imagePrompt: Two young chimpanzees tumbling together in play, mouths open in the classic ape 'play face,' warm jungle light

lessons.title: A social glue, not a joke response
lessons.body: Neuroscientist Robert Provine recorded 1,200 real-world laughs and made a startling discovery: only about 20 percent followed anything resembling a joke. Most laughter punctuates ordinary conversation — 'Hey!' 'Got to go.' 'See you later.' Laughter is primarily social signaling. It tells the other person: I'm safe. I'm with you. This is play, not threat.

visual.imagePrompt: Two friends deep in conversation at a café, one laughing while the other speaks, warm candid photography

lessons.title: The tickle paradox and contagious giggles
lessons.body: You can't tickle yourself — your brain predicts your own touch and cancels the response. But another person's fingers trigger both surprise and safety cues at once, and the laughter follows. Laughter is also contagious: fMRI studies show that just hearing a laugh activates the motor regions that produce your own. Canned sitcom laughter works because evolution wired you to join in.
lessons.funFact: A 2015 Oxford study found people who laugh together release measurable amounts of endorphins — literally painkillers — and bond more quickly than those who don't.

visual.imagePrompt: An fMRI brain scan with motor and auditory regions glowing in warm colors, scientifically elegant

lessons.title: An ancient language we still don't fully speak
lessons.body: Laughter predates words, punctuates our speech, bonds strangers, and releases painkillers. It's one of the oldest things our bodies do and one of the least understood. Next time you're laughing with someone, notice: you're using a communication system that's older than humanity. The joke is almost beside the point.

quiz.question: Roughly what percentage of everyday laughter follows something resembling a joke?
quiz.explanation: Provine's research showed that only about 20% of laughs follow jokes. Most punctuate ordinary conversation as social signaling.

quiz.question: Why can't you tickle yourself?
options: Your skin is less sensitive on your own hands
Your brain predicts your own touch and cancels the surprise response
Tickling requires two people by definition
quiz.explanation: Your brain's cerebellum anticipates self-generated motion and dampens the surprise signal that triggers tickling.

quiz.question: What is 'Duchenne laughter'?
options: Laughter from a specific comedy style
Spontaneous genuine laughter involving muscles around the eyes
quiz.explanation: Duchenne laughter is the genuine, involuntary kind — distinguished by the crinkling of eye muscles, which are very hard to fake.

sources.title: Reconstructing the Evolution of Laughter in Great Apes and Humans
